content:
10 min read
Just now

--

I used to think caching was the one thing you couldn’t mess up.
It’s not like SQL injection, where one misplaced quote destroys everything. It’s not like authentication, where a single missing check lets a stranger become an admin. Caching is just… speed. You store a result, you return it faster, you celebrate the reduced database load. What could possibly go wrong?
I had read the blog posts. I had seen the benchmarks. Adding Redis to a FastAPI app could cut response times from 200ms to 5ms. For an auth endpoint that was being hit thousands of times per hour, that felt like free performance.
It was free, alright. Free data for anyone who asked.
I deployed the change on a Thursday morning. By Thursday afternoon, users were seeing each other’s profiles, orders, and saved addresses. It took me six hours to notice. And in those six hours, I had turned my API into a public bulletin board.
The Setup: Why I Thought This Was Genius
My app had a standard login flow. User hits POST /auth/login with email and password. FastAPI validates the request, queries PostgreSQL, verifies the hash, generates a JWT, and returns the user object.
Simple. Secure. Boringly reliable.
But it was also slow. Every login required a database round-trip. Every token refresh required…
